sources.certVol1: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

sourceAudit.note: Content is original/paraphrased from the cited sources; questions are original. Verify command syntax against official Cisco docs.

objectives.title: Configure and verify VLANs (normal range) spanning multiple switches

ckus.title: VLAN
ckus.summary: A logical Layer 2 broadcast domain. Devices in the same VLAN communicate at L2 regardless of physical location; different VLANs require a router/L3 switch.

ckus.title: Access Port
ckus.summary: A switchport that belongs to exactly one VLAN and carries untagged traffic for an end device.

ckus.title: Voice VLAN
ckus.summary: A second VLAN on an access port for IP phones, so voice and data traffic are separated on the same physical port.

tiers.beginner: A VLAN lets one physical switch act like several separate switches. Ports put in VLAN 10 can talk to each other; ports in VLAN 20 are a separate group. To let VLAN 10 talk to VLAN 20 you need a router. You assign a normal end-device port to a VLAN by making it an “access” port.
tiers.intermediate: A VLAN is a logical broadcast domain. You create one with `vlan <id>` (and name it), then assign access ports with `switchport mode access` + `switchport access vlan <id>`. Devices in the same VLAN reach each other at Layer 2 across multiple switches (via trunks); devices in different VLANs need a Layer 3 device. VLAN 1 is the default for all ports — best practice is to move user traffic off VLAN 1. An IP phone can use a voice VLAN alongside the data VLAN on one port.
tiers.examReady: VLAN = logical broadcast domain. Create: `vlan 10` → `name SALES`. Assign an access port: `switchport mode access` + `switchport access vlan 10`. Verify with `show vlan brief`. Inter-VLAN traffic needs a router or L3 switch (SVI / router-on-a-stick). VLAN 1 is the default VLAN AND default native VLAN — best practice is to not use it for user data. Voice VLAN: `switchport voice vlan 20` puts phone traffic in a separate VLAN on the same access port. Normal range = 1–1005; extended = 1006–4094.

reading.definition: A **VLAN** is a logical Layer 2 broadcast domain. Same-VLAN devices talk at L2 (even across switches via trunks); **different VLANs need a router/L3 switch**. End-device ports are **access ports** assigned to one VLAN.
keyPoints: Assign access port: `switchport mode access` + `switchport access vlan <id>`.
Same VLAN = same broadcast domain; inter-VLAN needs L3.
VLAN 1 = default VLAN and default native VLAN — avoid for user traffic.
Voice VLAN carries phone traffic separately on an access port.
reading.realWorld: A trunk carries multiple VLANs between switches, so VLAN 10 can span the building. `show vlan brief` confirms port-to-VLAN mapping; a missing VLAN assignment is a top cause of “host can’t reach anyone.”
commonMistakes: Assigning a port to a VLAN that hasn’t been created (it may go inactive).
Expecting two VLANs to talk without a router/L3 switch.
Forgetting `switchport mode access` so the port may negotiate a trunk.
reading.advanced: Normal-range VLANs (1–1005) are stored in vlan.dat; extended (1006–4094) require certain VTP modes. A port in a deleted VLAN goes inactive until the VLAN exists again.

questions.question: A VLAN is best described as a…
questions.explanation: A VLAN is a logical broadcast domain at Layer 2.

questions.question: Hosts in VLAN 10 cannot reach hosts in VLAN 20. What is required?
choices: A trunk between the two hosts
questions.explanation: Traffic between VLANs must be routed by a Layer 3 device.

questions.question: Which commands assign a port to VLAN 10 as an access port?
choices: switchport mode access / switchport access vlan 10
questions.explanation: Set the mode to access, then assign the VLAN.

questions.question: Which command shows VLAN-to-port assignments?
questions.explanation: `show vlan brief` lists VLANs and their assigned ports.

questions.question: What is special about VLAN 1 by default?
choices: It is the only routable VLAN
It is the default VLAN and default native VLAN
questions.explanation: VLAN 1 is the default VLAN for all ports and the default native VLAN; best practice avoids it for user data.

questions.question: An IP phone and a PC share one switchport. How are their traffic types separated?
choices: A data VLAN + a voice VLAN on the port
questions.explanation: The access (data) VLAN plus a voice VLAN separate PC and phone traffic on one port.

questions.question: True or False: Devices in the same VLAN on two different switches can communicate at Layer 2 (given a trunk).
questions.explanation: True — a trunk lets a VLAN span switches at Layer 2.

questions.question: Which command creates VLAN 30?
questions.explanation: `vlan 30` in global config creates the VLAN.

flashcards.back: A logical Layer 2 broadcast domain; inter-VLAN traffic needs a router/L3 switch.

flashcards.front: Commands to make an access port in VLAN 10?
flashcards.back: switchport mode access / switchport access vlan 10.
Default VLAN + default native VLAN; avoid for user traffic.
Separates IP-phone traffic from PC data on the same access port.

commands.purpose: Assign an access port to a VLAN.
Display VLANs and their assigned ports.

glossary.definition: A logical Layer 2 broadcast domain configured on switches.
A switchport in one VLAN carrying untagged end-device traffic.
The untagged VLAN on a trunk; VLAN 1 by default.
A separate VLAN for IP-phone traffic on an access port.

mnemonics.title: Access port
mnemonics.explanation: Two commands in order: set mode, then assign the VLAN.

examTraps.trap: Thinking same-VLAN devices need a router.
examTraps.correction: Same VLAN = same broadcast domain, no routing needed; only inter-VLAN does.
Best practice moves user traffic off VLAN 1 and changes the native VLAN.

examTraps.trap: Assigning a port to an uncreated VLAN.
examTraps.correction: Create the VLAN first or the port may go inactive.

misconceptions.reality: VLANs segment broadcast domains for organization/security, not raw speed.
misconceptions.example: Smaller broadcast domains reduce flooding but don’t increase link rate.

misconceptions.misconception: A VLAN exists on only one switch.
misconceptions.reality: A VLAN can span many switches over trunks.
misconceptions.example: VLAN 10 on SW1 and SW2 is one broadcast domain via a trunk.

diagram.title: VLAN segmentation
annotations: VLAN10 ↔ VLAN20 needs a router/L3 switch.

steps.title: Frame in
steps.action: PC in VLAN10 sends a frame to another VLAN10 host.

steps.title: VLAN check
steps.action: Switch keeps the frame within VLAN10’s broadcast domain.

steps.title: Forward
steps.action: Frame is delivered out the destination’s VLAN10 port (no routing).

ckus.title: Trunking (802.1Q)
ckus.summary: A trunk carries traffic for multiple VLANs between switches using 802.1Q tagging — a 4-byte tag (12-bit VLAN ID) added to each frame.

ckus.title: Native VLAN
ckus.summary: The one VLAN whose frames cross an 802.1Q trunk UNtagged. Must match on both ends (default VLAN 1) or you get a native VLAN mismatch.

ckus.title: DTP
ckus.summary: Dynamic Trunking Protocol auto-negotiates trunk vs access; often disabled for security with `switchport nonegotiate`.

tiers.beginner: When two switches need to carry several VLANs over a single link between them, that link is a “trunk.” To keep VLANs separate, the switch adds a small tag to each frame saying which VLAN it belongs to. One VLAN — the “native” VLAN — is sent without a tag, and both ends must agree on which one it is.
tiers.intermediate: A trunk uses 802.1Q to tag frames with their VLAN ID (a 4-byte tag) so multiple VLANs share one link. Configure with `switchport mode trunk`; restrict VLANs with `switchport trunk allowed vlan <list>`; set the untagged native VLAN with `switchport trunk native vlan <id>` (must match both ends). DTP can auto-form trunks but is commonly disabled (`switchport nonegotiate`) to prevent VLAN-hopping. Verify with `show interfaces trunk`.
tiers.examReady: Trunk = 802.1Q-tagged link carrying multiple VLANs between switches. Config: `switchport mode trunk`, optionally `switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20`, and `switchport trunk native vlan 99`. The native VLAN crosses UNtagged and must match on both ends (mismatch → CDP warning, traffic leakage). 802.1Q tag = 4 bytes inserted into the Ethernet frame, holding the 12-bit VLAN ID (so 4094 usable VLANs). DTP auto-negotiates trunking; disable with `switchport nonegotiate` for security. Verify: `show interfaces trunk` (mode, native VLAN, allowed/active VLANs).

reading.definition: A **trunk** carries multiple VLANs between switches using **802.1Q** tagging (a 4-byte tag with the VLAN ID). One **native VLAN** crosses **untagged** and must match on both ends.
keyPoints: 802.1Q tag = 4 bytes, 12-bit VLAN ID (up to 4094 VLANs).
Native VLAN is untagged; must match both ends (default VLAN 1).
Restrict VLANs with `switchport trunk allowed vlan <list>`.
DTP auto-negotiates trunks; disable with `switchport nonegotiate`.
reading.realWorld: A native-VLAN mismatch shows up as a CDP error and can leak traffic between VLANs. Pruning the allowed VLAN list limits broadcast scope and is a security best practice.
commonMistakes: Native VLAN mismatch between the two trunk ends.
Leaving the native VLAN as 1 (security risk).
Allowing all VLANs on a trunk that only needs a few.
Relying on DTP auto-negotiation on untrusted links.
reading.advanced: ISL is the legacy Cisco trunking protocol; 802.1Q is the standard and the only one on the exam in practice. A double-tagging attack abuses the native VLAN — hence the advice to use a dedicated, unused native VLAN.

questions.question: What does a trunk link do?
choices: Connects a PC to a switch
questions.explanation: A trunk carries traffic for multiple VLANs between switches using 802.1Q.

questions.question: How big is the 802.1Q tag added to a frame?
questions.explanation: The 802.1Q tag is 4 bytes and contains the 12-bit VLAN ID.

questions.question: On an 802.1Q trunk, how is the native VLAN’s traffic sent?
questions.explanation: Native VLAN frames cross the trunk untagged.

questions.question: Two trunked switches log a native VLAN mismatch. What is the likely effect?
choices: Traffic from those VLANs can leak/merge
questions.explanation: A native VLAN mismatch can merge the two native VLANs’ traffic and triggers a CDP warning.

questions.question: Which command forces a port to trunk?
questions.explanation: `switchport mode trunk` sets the port to trunking.

questions.question: Which command disables DTP auto-negotiation on a port?
questions.explanation: `switchport nonegotiate` stops DTP, a hardening best practice.

questions.question: Which command shows trunk mode, native VLAN, and allowed VLANs?
questions.explanation: `show interfaces trunk` lists trunking details.

questions.question: Which command limits a trunk to VLANs 10 and 20?
questions.explanation: `switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20` prunes the trunk.

flashcards.front: What is a trunk and what protocol tags it?
flashcards.back: A link carrying multiple VLANs between switches; 802.1Q tags frames with the VLAN ID.

flashcards.front: Size and content of the 802.1Q tag?
How is the native VLAN carried, and what must match?
flashcards.back: Untagged; the native VLAN ID must match on both trunk ends.

flashcards.front: Command to restrict allowed VLANs on a trunk?

commands.purpose: Configure the port as an 802.1Q trunk.
Set the untagged native VLAN (match both ends).
Show trunk mode, native VLAN, and allowed/active VLANs.

glossary.definition: A switch link carrying multiple VLANs using 802.1Q tagging.
The IEEE VLAN tagging standard; inserts a 4-byte tag with the VLAN ID.
The VLAN sent untagged on a trunk; must match both ends.

mnemonics.title: Native = naked
mnemonics.explanation: The native VLAN is the one frame type sent without an 802.1Q tag.

examTraps.correction: Native VLAN must match on both ends or traffic can leak (CDP warns).
The native VLAN is untagged; all others are tagged.

examTraps.trap: Leaving DTP on for untrusted ports.
examTraps.correction: Disable DTP with switchport nonegotiate to prevent VLAN hopping.

misconceptions.misconception: A trunk is faster than an access port.
misconceptions.reality: A trunk simply carries multiple VLANs; speed depends on the physical link.
misconceptions.example: A 1G trunk and 1G access port have the same link rate.

misconceptions.reality: 802.1Q inserts a 4-byte tag into the existing frame; it does not wrap it.
misconceptions.example: The tag sits after the source MAC.

diagram.title: Trunk between switches
annotations: One link carries many VLANs, each frame tagged with its VLAN ID.

steps.title: Frame enters SW1
steps.action: A VLAN10 frame arrives at SW1.

steps.title: Tag
steps.action: SW1 inserts an 802.1Q tag with VLAN ID 10 before sending on the trunk.

steps.title: SW2 reads tag
steps.action: SW2 reads the tag, removes it, and places the frame in VLAN10.

steps.title: Deliver
steps.action: Frame is forwarded out a VLAN10 access port.

objectives.title: Interpret basic operations of Rapid PVST+ Spanning Tree Protocol

ckus.title: Spanning Tree Protocol
ckus.summary: Prevents Layer 2 loops by blocking redundant switch paths, leaving one active path to the root bridge.

ckus.title: Root Bridge Election
ckus.summary: The switch with the lowest Bridge ID (priority + MAC) becomes root; all others find the lowest-cost path to it. Default priority 32768.

ckus.title: PortFast & BPDU Guard
ckus.summary: PortFast skips listening/learning on access ports for fast host connectivity; BPDU Guard err-disables a PortFast port that receives a BPDU (rogue switch).

tiers.beginner: If you connect switches in a loop for redundancy, frames can circle forever and crash the network. Spanning Tree fixes this by automatically blocking the extra paths, leaving just one active route. If a link fails, it unblocks a backup path. One switch is elected the “root,” and every other switch keeps its best path toward it.
tiers.intermediate: STP prevents Layer 2 loops by electing a root bridge (lowest Bridge ID = priority + MAC) and blocking redundant links. Each non-root switch has one root port (best path to root) and each segment has one designated port; other ports block. Port roles/states converge so exactly one loop-free path exists. Rapid PVST+ (Cisco default) runs a per-VLAN rapid spanning tree that converges in seconds using proposal/agreement. PortFast on access ports skips the listening/learning delay; BPDU Guard shuts a PortFast port if it unexpectedly receives a BPDU.
tiers.examReady: STP (802.1D) blocks redundant L2 paths to prevent loops. Root bridge = lowest Bridge ID (priority + MAC); default priority 32768; lower wins (ties broken by lowest MAC). Roles: Root Port (one per non-root switch, lowest cost to root), Designated Port (one per segment, forwards), Non-Designated/Alternate (blocks). Path cost by speed (e.g. 10G=2, 1G=4, 100M=19, 10M=100). Rapid PVST+ = Cisco default, per-VLAN, states discarding/learning/forwarding, fast convergence via proposal/agreement. PortFast → access ports skip listening/learning; BPDU Guard → err-disables a PortFast port that receives a BPDU. Set root: `spanning-tree vlan 1 root primary` (or lower the priority). Verify: `show spanning-tree`.

reading.definition: STP prevents **Layer 2 loops** by blocking redundant paths, leaving one path to the **root bridge** (lowest Bridge ID). **Rapid PVST+** (Cisco default) converges in seconds. **PortFast/BPDU Guard** speed up and protect access ports.
keyPoints: Root bridge = lowest Bridge ID (priority + MAC); default priority `32768`.
Roles: root port (per switch), designated port (per segment), others block.
Lower path cost wins; cost by link speed (1G=`4`, 100M=`19`).
Rapid PVST+ = Cisco default; states discarding/learning/forwarding.
PortFast skips listening/learning on access ports.
BPDU Guard err-disables a PortFast port that receives a BPDU.
reading.realWorld: Lower a switch’s priority (`spanning-tree vlan 1 priority 4096`) to make it root deterministically. `show spanning-tree` reveals the root, port roles, and which ports are blocking — the first stop when redundant links misbehave.
commonMistakes: Assuming the highest-priority number wins (lowest BID wins).
Enabling PortFast on a switch-to-switch link (loop risk).
Forgetting BPDU Guard, leaving PortFast ports open to rogue switches.
Confusing root port (toward root) with designated port (per segment).
reading.advanced: Rapid PVST+ uses proposal/agreement to converge without timers in most cases. Root Guard prevents a downstream switch from becoming root; Loop Guard protects against unidirectional link failures.

questions.question: What problem does STP solve?
questions.explanation: STP prevents Layer 2 loops by blocking redundant paths.

questions.question: How is the root bridge chosen?
questions.explanation: The lowest Bridge ID (priority, then MAC) wins the root election.

questions.question: What is the default STP bridge priority?
questions.explanation: Default priority is 32768 (lower is preferred).

questions.question: Each non-root switch has exactly one of which port type toward the root?
questions.explanation: Each non-root switch has one root port — its lowest-cost path to root.

questions.question: PortFast should be enabled on which ports?
questions.explanation: PortFast belongs on access ports to hosts; on switch links it risks loops.

questions.question: A PortFast access port receives a BPDU with BPDU Guard enabled. What happens?
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard err-disables the port, protecting against a rogue switch.

questions.question: What is the Cisco default spanning-tree mode?
questions.explanation: Rapid PVST+ is the Cisco default — per-VLAN rapid spanning tree.

questions.question: Which command makes a switch the root for VLAN 1 by lowering its priority?
questions.explanation: `spanning-tree vlan 1 root primary` lowers the priority so this switch becomes root.

flashcards.back: Blocks redundant L2 paths to prevent switching loops; unblocks on failure.

flashcards.front: How is the root bridge elected?
flashcards.back: Lowest Bridge ID = priority (default 32768) then MAC.
Root port (per switch), designated port (per segment), blocking/alternate.
Skips listening/learning so access ports forward immediately.
Err-disables a PortFast port that receives a BPDU (rogue switch protection).

flashcards.front: Cisco default STP mode + verify command?

commands.purpose: Show the root bridge, port roles/states, and costs.
Lower priority so this switch becomes the root for a VLAN.
Enable PortFast on an access port.

glossary.definition: L2 protocol that blocks redundant paths to prevent loops.
The switch with the lowest Bridge ID; reference point of the tree.
A non-root switch’s lowest-cost port toward the root.
Feature letting access ports skip listening/learning.
Err-disables a PortFast port that receives a BPDU.

mnemonics.title: Lowest wins
mnemonics.explanation: Priority first (default 32768), then MAC — lowest is root.

examTraps.correction: LOWEST Bridge ID (priority then MAC) becomes root.
PortFast belongs only on access/host ports; on switch links it risks loops.
Root port = best path to root (per switch); designated = forwarding port per segment.

misconceptions.reality: Classic STP blocks redundant links (one active path); EtherChannel or per-VLAN roots are needed to use both.
misconceptions.example: A blocked link carries no data until the primary fails.

misconceptions.reality: STP is Layer 2 only; routing loops are handled by L3 mechanisms (TTL, split horizon).
misconceptions.example: STP has no effect on IP routing.

diagram.title: STP blocks one redundant path
annotations: The A–B link is blocked to break the loop.

steps.title: Elect root
steps.action: Switches exchange BPDUs; lowest Bridge ID becomes root.

steps.title: Choose root ports
steps.action: Each non-root switch picks its lowest-cost port to root.

steps.title: Choose designated
steps.action: Each segment elects one designated (forwarding) port.

steps.title: Block the rest
steps.action: Remaining ports block, leaving a single loop-free tree.

lab.title: Configure VLANs, Access Ports, and an 802.1Q Trunk
lab.scenario: SW1 and SW2 each host PCs in two departments — Sales (VLAN 10) and Engineering (VLAN 20). You will create both VLANs on each switch, assign access ports, and configure the link between SW1 and SW2 as an 802.1Q trunk so VLAN 10 and 20 traffic can both cross between switches.
learningGoals: Create VLANs and assign descriptive names.
Assign access ports to the correct VLAN with switchport mode access.
Configure a trunk port and verify the native VLAN matches on both ends.
Verify VLAN membership and trunk status.

tasks.title: Create VLANs on SW1
tasks.instruction: Create VLAN 10 (name Sales) and VLAN 20 (name Engineering) on SW1.

tasks.title: Create VLANs on SW2
tasks.instruction: Create the same two VLANs (10 Sales, 20 Engineering) on SW2.

tasks.title: Assign access ports on SW1
tasks.instruction: Set Gi0/1 as an access port in VLAN 10 (PC1) and Gi0/2 as an access port in VLAN 20 (PC2).

tasks.title: Assign access port on SW2
tasks.instruction: Set Gi0/1 as an access port in VLAN 10 (PC3).

tasks.title: Configure the trunk on SW1
tasks.instruction: Configure Gi0/3 (link to SW2) as an 802.1Q trunk carrying VLANs 10 and 20, with the default native VLAN 1.

tasks.title: Configure the trunk on SW2
tasks.instruction: Configure Gi0/3 (link to SW1) as a matching 802.1Q trunk carrying VLANs 10 and 20.

successCriteria: show vlan brief lists VLAN 10 (Sales) and VLAN 20 (Engineering) with the correct access ports.
show interfaces trunk shows Gi0/3 as a trunk on both switches, allowing VLANs 10 and 20.
PC1 (VLAN 10 on SW1) can ping PC3 (VLAN 10 on SW2) across the trunk.
The native VLAN (1) matches on both ends of the trunk — no mismatch warning.
failureCriteria: Forgetting to add the VLAN to the trunk allowed list → that VLAN's traffic is dropped at the trunk.
Mismatched native VLANs on the two trunk ports → native VLAN mismatch error/log.
Leaving Gi0/3 in access mode → only one VLAN crosses, others are blocked.
Assigning an access port to a VLAN that was never created → port goes into an inactive VLAN.
commonMistakes: Typing `switchport access vlan 10` without first creating VLAN 10 — works, but the VLAN shows as "inactive" until created.
Forgetting `switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q` on switches that support multiple encapsulations (ISL legacy).
Leaving the allowed-VLAN list at its default (all VLANs) when the design calls for restricting it.
Mismatching the native VLAN between the two ends of a trunk.

topology.title: VLAN + trunk topology
notes: Only the trunk (Gi0/3 on each switch) carries tagged frames.

verificationChecks.expectedResult: VLAN 10 (Sales) and VLAN 20 (Engineering) listed with Gi0/1 and Gi0/2 respectively.
Gi0/3 listed as a trunk, allowed VLANs 10,20.
Ping succeeds — both in VLAN 10, reachable across the trunk.

failureChecks.expectedFailure: VLAN 20 missing from the allowed list
failureChecks.reason: If `switchport trunk allowed vlan` excludes 20, VLAN 20 traffic cannot cross the trunk.

diagram.title: Tagged vs untagged frames across the trunk
annotations: SW1 adds an 802.1Q tag (VLAN 10) before sending the frame across the trunk.
SW2 strips the tag before delivering the frame to PC3, which never sees tags.

steps.title: Frame from PC1
steps.action: PC1 (VLAN 10) sends an untagged frame to SW1.

steps.title: Tag added
steps.action: SW1 tags the frame with VLAN 10 and sends it out the Gi0/3 trunk.

steps.title: Tag read
steps.action: SW2 reads the VLAN 10 tag and looks up its VLAN 10 MAC table.

steps.title: Untagged to PC3
steps.action: SW2 strips the tag and forwards the untagged frame out the Gi0/1 access port to PC3.

packetFlows.title: VLAN not in the allowed list is dropped

steps.title: Frame from VLAN 20
steps.action: PC2 (VLAN 20) sends a frame to SW1.

steps.title: Allowed-list check
steps.action: If VLAN 20 is not in `switchport trunk allowed vlan` on Gi0/3, the frame is dropped at the trunk.
